data.title: As a practitioner, out-of-scope review feedback routes to implementation-plan between batches
data.description:
## Description

**What**: When code review requests modifications to files not in the original task file impact, the sprint orchestrator routes the finding to implementation-plan to create new tasks. This fires only between batches to avoid mid-batch conflicts.
**Why**: Today, out-of-scope review feedback is either defended or rejected. There is no path to accept the feedback and create tasks for it, losing valuable reviewer insights.
**Scope**:
- IN: Out-of-scope file detection (compare review findings against task file impact), routing to implementation-plan, between-batch timing constraint, REPLAN_TRIGGER: review comment
- OUT: Review defense logic changes (existing), brainstorm escalation

## Done Definitions
- When this story is complete, when a reviewer requests changes to files outside the original task scope and the orchestrator accepts the finding, sprint routes to implementation-plan to create new tasks for those files
  ← Satisfies: "When code review requests modifications to files not listed in the original task file impact"
- When this story is complete, review escalation fires only between sub-agent batches, not during active batch execution
  ← Satisfies: "This escalation fires only between batches, not mid-batch"
- When this story is complete, a REPLAN_TRIGGER: review comment is recorded on the epic
  ← Satisfies: "each trigger records a structured REPLAN_TRIGGER"
- When this story is complete, unit tests written and passing for all new or modified logic

## Considerations
- [Reliability] Must fire only between batches — mid-batch injection creates task overlap with in-flight agents

## Escalation Policy
**Escalation policy**: Escalate to the user whenever you do not have high confidence in your understanding of the work, approach, or intent. "High confidence" means clear evidence from the codebase or ticket context — not inference or reasonable assumption. When in doubt, stop and ask rather than guess.
